title: The Mystery of the Shimamura Family
description: Shimamura's father reflects on three strange things: Hougetsu's unusual cheerfulness, the glowing girl who won't leave, and his wife's carefree attitude. A playful glimpse into the Shimamura household.
content:
Lately, there are three things I've found strange.

The first is that Hougetsu has been looking unusually cheerful these days.

'What do you think, dear?'

''Bout what?'

My wife, sitting on the floor with her back hunched, was busy trimming her toenails. She didn't seem to be taking in half of what I was saying.

'If she's happy, that's a good thing, isn't it?'

'I suppose so, but still…'

Every year during the New Year holidays, Hougetsu usually just sleeps all day, barely moving from her futon. But this time, I often see her walking around, even smiling as she passes down the hallway. It's… different. And that's what got me wondering.

Ever since she entered high school, she's seemed to regain that gentle air she had back in elementary school. During her middle school years, she went through quite the rebellious phase—had us a bit worried, honestly.

'I'm always happy, you know,' my wife said in a sing-song voice.

'Ah, how enviable,' I replied.

She was clearly not using her brain right now. I was ready to drop the conversation when she suddenly piped up again.

'Maybe she's got herself a boyfriend?'

'A boyfriend… huh.'

So that's what you think, I see. …That's what you think.

'Or maybe a girlfriend.'

'…A girlfriend?'

I tilted my head. What's that supposed to mean? My wife gave a low, creepy chuckle.

'That Adachi girl's a bit suspicious, don't you think?'

'Suspicious, huh?'

'Very suspicious. You are too, by the way.'

'Adachi… you mean that kid from the other day?'

'Yep. The quiet one.'

Her and Hougetsu… hmm. Hmmm?

'It's a complicated world.'

'Yeah, who knows. Anyway, mystery solved.'

And with that half-hearted conclusion, my wife wrapped things up. I could only look around the room, muttering 'huh' and 'hmm' under my breath, a bit restless. But after a while, I thought—well, if she's happy, that's what matters.

Now, the second thing: the kid my wife's been holding under her arm all this time.

'She looks like she's glowing.'

'Sure is,' my wife said flatly.

'Sparkly, isn't she,' the glowing kid chimed in casually.

There's this strange little girl who's just… always around.

'I can shine even brighter, you know.'

'Don't. It's blinding,' my wife said without looking up.

It must be hard to clip nails while holding her like that.

'If I don't keep her still, she'll sneak off to open the fridge again.'

'The kitchen is so fun!' the glowing kid said, wiggling her hands and feet energetically.

'Why don't you all look inside the fridge more often?'

'Because the food disappears when we do!'

'Still… why is she glowing?' I asked.

'No clue. But sometimes she's useful. She lights up the place at night.'

'Peee-kaaa~,' said the kid lazily, glowing even brighter.

That pale blue light… I feel like I've seen it somewhere before. Or maybe not.

'Just think of her as a talking cat that decided to move in,' my wife said.

'That's a pleasant thought, but… is it really okay?'

I do like cats, but this one's clearly from someone else's house. Doesn't their family say anything?

'She's not a bad kid,' my wife replied.

'Is that so?'

'Our daughters trust her.'

My wife said it so matter-of-factly. That's just how she is.

'Then I guess that's fine.'

'Yup.'

'Oh, Yachi's here,' she said suddenly.

As soon as our daughter poked her head into the room, my wife tossed the glowing kid toward her.

'Here, take her with you.'

The kid soared through the air—literally flew—and after slowly spinning once around my daughter's head, landed soundlessly. In her wake, a trail of blue particles drifted lazily through the air.

I couldn't even close my mouth.

'Well then, sir, shall we go to the kitchen?'

'No, we shall not.'

'Oh?'

And off they went, hand in hand, disappearing down the hall.

'...They look like they're having fun too.'

'That's a good thing,' my wife said.

'I suppose it is.'

'I'm having fun too, y'know,' she added.

'That's enough for you.'

She cackled—her usual weird laugh—and I thought, not for the first time, that I'm glad our daughters didn't inherit that.

'What about the third one?'

'Hmm?'

'You said there were three strange things, remember?'

Freed from holding the glowing kid, she went back to clipping her nails—snip, snip, snip.

'I'm surprised you were even listening.'

'I'm full of surprises,' she said proudly.

Probably not the kind of surprise I had in mind—more like one of those bamboo shoots that suddenly spring up out of nowhere.

'Well, go on then, what's the third?'

'That wasn't the plan… but fine. It's you. You seem more cheerful than usual.'

'Do I? Can't say I've noticed.'

Even when she has no clue what's going on, she says it with a smile.

My slightly odd wife kept humming as she continued clipping her nails.
